dublinCore.dc:title: 119 HR 9142 IH: Prohibiting Adversarial Patents Act of 2026
dublinCore.dc:rights: Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain.

action.action-desc: Mr. Fitzgerald (for himself, Mr. Moolenaar, and Mr. Issa) introduced the following bill; which was referred to the Committee on the Judiciary

form.official-title: To amend title 35, United States Code, to limit the ability of persons who pose a threat to national security to receive and enforce patents, and for other purposes.

section.text: This Act may be cited as the Prohibiting Adversarial Patents Act of 2026.

section.header: Limitation on ability of persons who pose a threat to national security to receive and enforce patents

subsection.text: Chapter 10 of title 35, United States Code, is amended by adding at the end the following:

section.header: Limitation on ability of persons who pose a threat to national security to receive and enforce patents

subsection.text: Notwithstanding any other provision of law—

paragraph.text: a person may not be issued a United States patent for an invention if that person (or any subsidiary, affiliate, successor, assign, legal representative, or privy of such person)—

subparagraph.text: is an entity on the Non-SDN Chinese Military-Industrial Complex Companies List (NS–CMIC List) maintained by the Office of Foreign Assets Control of the Department of the Treasury under Executive Order 14032 (86 Fed. Reg. 30145; relating to addressing the threat from securities investments that finance certain companies of the People’s Republic of China), or any successor order;

is identified as a Chinese military company in the most recent annual report submitted pursuant to section 1260H of the William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021 (Public Law 116–283; 10 U.S.C. 113 note); or

produces or provides communications equipment or service on the list published by the Federal Communications Commission under section 2(a) of the Secure and Trusted Communications Networks Act of 2019 (47 U.S.C. 1601(a));

paragraph.text: any United States patent issued to a person described under paragraph (1) shall be unenforceable; and

no procedure, including any procedure under the Patent Prosecution Highway Program, may be used to conduct an expedited review an application for a patent filed by a person described under paragraph (1).
The President may waive the application of the prohibition under subsection (a) with respect to a person for successive periods of not more than 180 days each if the President—

subparagraph.text: determines that there is not an essential security interest in applying the prohibition under subsection (a) with respect to a person; and
submits, not less than 30 days before waiving such prohibition, a report to the Committee on the Judiciary of the Senate and the Committee on the Judiciary of the House of Representatives—

clause.text: a specific and detailed rationale for the determination that there is not an essential security interest in applying the prohibition with respect to that person;
a description of the activity that resulted in the person being included on a list or in a report described under subparagraphs (A) through (C);
an assessment of the significance of such activity to the national security interests of the United States or allies of the United States; and

an explanation of any effort made by the United States to secure the cooperation of the government with primary jurisdiction over the person or the location where the activity described in clause (ii) occurred in to terminate, as appropriate, or penalize such activity.

paragraph.text: The President shall ensure that in granting any waiver under this subsection, the President exercises such authority in a manner that is consistent with international obligations.

subsection.header: No effect on patent application; ownership and term of patent
subsection.text: The application of the prohibition under subsection (a), or the grant of any waiver under subsection (b), to a person shall not affect—

paragraph.text: the ability of such person file a patent application;
except as provided in subsection (a)(3), the examination of any such application; or
the ownership or term of any patent owned by such person.

subsection.text: The table of sections for chapter 10 of title 35, United States Code, is amended by inserting after the item relating to section 105 the following:

toc.toc-entry: 106. Limitation on ability of persons who pose a threat to national security to receive and enforce patents.
